stories.title: Alston & Bird Relocates NYC Offices to 51W52 in 170K-SF Lease
stories.excerpt: Newmark has arranged a new 169,664-square-foot lease on behalf of affiliates of Harbor Group International (HGI), at 51 W. 52nd St., bringing the iconic Midtown Manhattan office tower to full occupancy. International law firm Alston & Bird LLP has signed a 15-year lease and will relocate its New York offices from 90 Park Ave. Executive vice chairman Howard Hersch, vice chairmen Greg Conen and Scott Klau, executive managing director Erik Harris and managing directors Jennifer Schreiber and Zach Weil serve as leasing agents for the property and represented HGI in negotiations. Savills’ Brad Walk, Matthew Barlow, David Goldstein, Joseph Learner, Matthew Brainard and Laura Whelan represented the tenant. “We are proud to announce that our flagship property, 51W52, is now fully leased to a roster of prominent tenants, all of whom match the building’s prestige,” said HGI chairman and CEO Jordan E. Slone. “The success of 51W52 validates our strong faith in the resilience of the New York office market, especially for the best trophy properties in the best locations.” The post Alston & Bird Relocates NYC Offices to 51W52 in 170K-SF Lease appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Alston & Bird signs 169,664-sf lease at 51 W. 52nd, Manhattan office tower now fully leased.
Major law firm relocation from 90 Park Ave signals trophy office demand in Midtown Manhattan.
HGI chairman cites 'resilience' of NYC office market; validates high-end trophy property investment thesis.
stories.summary: International law firm Alston & Bird has leased 169,664 square feet at 51 W. 52nd Street in Manhattan on a 15-year term, filling the last vacancy at the trophy Midtown office tower and bringing it to 100% occupancy. The lease represents a major institutional tenant's bet on Manhattan's best-in-class office properties. This signals sustained demand for premier office space in prime Manhattan markets despite broader office headwinds.
stories.impact: In Midtown Manhattan, trophy office leasing activity by major institutional tenants (law firms, financial services, tech) continues to support valuations and cap rates for best-in-class buildings. This validates the flight-to-quality thesis: weaker assets absorb sublease supply while premier properties tighten, supporting rents and reducing risk for lenders. However, multifamily developers should note: strong office demand in Manhattan does NOT automatically drive apartment demand—law firm employees are often high-income earners who either own or demand luxury rental stock, which is more supply-constrained. This favors already-built multifamily assets over new construction feasibility in core Manhattan.

stories.title: Walker & Dunlop Arranges Wells Fargo Refi for Rosemead Center
stories.excerpt: Walker & Dunlop has arranged a $46-million refinance loan for Rosemead Place, a prominent retail center in Rosemead. The Walker & Dunlop Capital Markets Real Estate Finance team led by Trevor Fase and Lynn Pearson secured the fixed-rate, interest-only permanent debt loan provided by Joel Minugh and James Ko of Wells Fargo on behalf of a family office. Located at the intersection of I-10 and Rosemead Boulevard, the 336,718-square-foot retail center features a diverse mix of national tenants including Target, LA Fitness, Ross Dress for Less, PetSmart, ULTA Beauty, and Dollar Tree. It serves as a primary retail destination for the surrounding community, according to Walker & Dunlop. “Well-located, anchored retail assets like Rosemead Place continue to be viewed extremely favorably by a wide range of capital providers, including insurance companies, CMBS lenders, and regional and national banks,” said Fase. “Despite several moving parts, including near-term lease rollover, we successfully closed the transaction in less than 60 days.” The post Walker & Dunlop Arranges Wells Fargo Refi for Rosemead Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Wells Fargo refi $46M for Rosemead Place retail, 336,718-sf center in Los Angeles County.
Anchor tenants (Target, LA Fitness, Ross, ULTA) and strong capital appetite signal retail confidence.
Fixed-rate permanent debt in <60 days shows lender comfort with well-anchored retail fundamentals.
stories.summary: Walker & Dunlop secured a $46 million refinance for Rosemead Place, a 336,718-square-foot retail center anchored by Target, LA Fitness, Ross Dress for Less, and ULTA Beauty in Rosemead, Los Angeles County. The fixed-rate, interest-only permanent loan closed in under 60 days, reflecting strong lender appetite for well-located, anchored retail. The center benefits from freeway visibility (I-10 and Rosemead Boulevard intersection) and serves as a primary retail destination.
stories.impact: Retail credit conditions remain accessible for stabilized, anchored properties in secondary LA County submarkets, suggesting lenders view durable-goods retail (apparel, fitness, beauty) as recession-resistant. For multifamily developers, this signals continued foot traffic and economic vitality in the Rosemead area (prime for apartment demand generation), but the ease of retail financing also means competitive supply of retail-anchored mixed-use projects. Focus underwriting on-site demographics and whether the retail drives local job creation and renter absorption.

stories.title: Bronx Bank’s Flagship Branch Goes for $16M in 1031 Exchange
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap finalized the sale of a net-leased bank property occupied by the headquarters and flagship branch of Ponce Bank, located at 2244 Westchester Ave. in the Bronx. The asset sold for $16,250,000. “This property drew tremendous interest as it was a true management-free NNN investment with annual increases,” said Steven Siegel of Marcus & Millichap’s New York office. “Unlike most other single-tenant properties, this was a large parcel with significant future development potential, in addition to being right by the subway. We created a competitive bidding environment, and in the final stage, three buyers aggressively pushed the price up and the cap down to 6.1%. Ultimately, we selected an all-cash 1031 buyer.” Siegel exclusively marketed the property on behalf of the seller, Arc Trust. The buyer, completing a 1031 exchange, was procured by Judson Kauffman of Surmont. Ponce Bank has occupied the property for 25 years. The post Bronx Bank’s Flagship Branch Goes for $16M in 1031 Exchange appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Ponce Bank headquarters building in Bronx sells for $16.25M on 6.1% cap rate.
22-acre parcel with subway access and 'significant future development potential' attracts competitive bidding.
1031 exchange buyer secures asset; bank held for 25 years, signaling long-term NNN stability.
stories.summary: A net-leased, single-tenant bank building housing Ponce Bank's headquarters at 2244 Westchester Ave. in the Bronx sold for $16.25 million at a 6.1% cap rate. The asset attracted strong competitive interest from three final bidders, all-cash 1031 buyer prevailed. The 22-acre parcel offers significant redevelopment optionality and is adjacent to transit, with Ponce Bank having occupied for 25 years.
stories.impact: In the Bronx, transit-accessible land parcels with dual-use potential (stabilized NNN income plus redevelopment optionality) are attracting institutional 1031 capital, pushing cap rates lower and signaling speculative upside for ground-up residential or mixed-use projects. The low 6.1% cap on a single-tenant bank lease suggests appetite for land plays near subway infrastructure. Multifamily developers should monitor Westchester Ave. corridors for similar assemblage opportunities; the 1031 buyer's acquisition may pre-stage a future residential or mixed-use conversion. Strong demand signal for Bronx development sites.

stories.title: Rancho Cucamonga Neighborhood Retail Sells for $6M Off-Market
stories.excerpt: Progressive Real Estate Partners has closed the $6-million, off-market acquisition of Allmark Plaza, 16,926-square-foot multi-tenant unanchored neighborhood retail center located at 10060-10080 Arrow Route in Rancho Cucamonga, to an Orange County-based private investor. VP of investment sales Lance Mordachini and VP of retail leasing and sales Monica Dal Bianco-West represented the buyer. The seller, Allmark Property Management, represented itself. “The buyer was attracted to Allmark Plaza because it checked the boxes that matter most in today’s market: strong historical occupancy, replaceable rents, a diverse rent roll, and a quality Rancho Cucamonga location,” said Mordachini. “At a $6 million purchase price and a 6.91% cap rate, we viewed this as a compelling opportunity to acquire a well-located, fully occupied retail asset with durable long-term fundamentals.” Built in 1986, Allmark Plaza offers convenient access to the I-10 and I-210 Freeways. The center is 100% occupied by a diverse tenant mix ranging from Domino’s Pizza to a dental office. The post Rancho Cucamonga Neighborhood Retail Sells for $6M Off-Market appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: 16,926-sf unanchored neighborhood retail in Rancho Cucamonga sells for $6M at 6.91% cap.
100% occupied with diverse tenants (Domino's, dental office); strong historical occupancy cited.
Location near I-10 and I-210; buyer values 'replaceable rents' and durable fundamentals.
stories.summary: Allmark Plaza, a 16,926-square-foot unanchored neighborhood retail center in Rancho Cucamonga, sold off-market for $6 million at a 6.91% cap rate. The 1986-built center is 100% occupied by a diverse tenant mix with convenient freeway access (I-10 and I-210). The buyer emphasized strong historical occupancy and 'replaceable rents' as key investment drivers.
stories.impact: In Inland Empire/Rancho Cucamonga, neighborhood retail is transacting at healthy cap rates (6.9%), reflecting lender and investor confidence in submarket demographics and consumer spending. For multifamily developers, this underscores continued demand for daily-use retail anchors and signifies a healthy, stable submarket with durable household income. Opportunities exist to identify retail-anchored mixed-use development sites or to acquire nearby land for multifamily projects confident in the retail's ability to drive foot traffic and renter draw. Freeway-accessible markets like Rancho Cucamonga remain favorable for apartment construction cost curves and tenant acquisition.

stories.title: 22-Property NJ Workforce Portfolio Refinanced for $38M
stories.excerpt: Benmark Capital recently closed on a $38-million loan to refinance a 22-building workforce housing portfolio in Paterson, NJ. The financing was secured by Ania Management, led by Founder and CEO Charles Florio. The transaction was arranged by Marc Waldman of Kentmoore Capital. Comprising 203 residential units and six commercial units across 22 multifamily and mixed-use buildings, the workforce housing portfolio is approximately 99% occupied. The financing package was provided to support the sponsor’s refinancing strategy and position the asset for future agency financing. The transaction spanned 22 separate buildings to be coordinated and completed simultaneously. “The Paterson Portfolio was a time-sensitive, highly structured financing that demanded precision and certainty of execution,” said Mark Simon, CEO of Benmark Capital. “Charles Florio and the Ania Management team are best-in-class operators and a trusted repeat borrower, and we were proud to once again deliver a customized capital solution tailored to both their immediate objectives and long-term strategy.” The post 22-Property NJ Workforce Portfolio Refinanced for $38M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Benmark Capital closes $38M refi for 22-building workforce portfolio in Paterson, NJ; 203 units.
Portfolio 99% occupied; structured loan across 22 simultaneous closings; positioned for agency financing.
Founder Charles Florio is 'best-in-class operator'; transaction supports sponsor's long-term strategy.
stories.summary: Benmark Capital arranged a $38 million refinance for a 22-property workforce housing portfolio (203 residential + 6 commercial units) in Paterson, New Jersey, owned and operated by Ania Management founder Charles Florio. The portfolio is approximately 99% occupied. The structured financing was designed to position the portfolio for future agency financing and support the sponsor's strategic objectives.
stories.impact: In the New York City periphery (Paterson, NJ), workforce housing portfolios continue to attract debt capital, especially from specialty lenders, due to high occupancy and consistent operator track records. For multifamily developers, this signals strong institutional appetite for workforce and affordable housing in secondary markets near major metros, particularly where operators have institutional credibility. The bridge financing to agency debt pathway indicates lenders expect regulatory support for workforce housing. Consider acquisition of similar workforce portfolios in NJ/PA suburbs for refinance and hold strategies; agency debt availability is robust.

stories.title: California Voters Will Decide If Local Taxes Should Be Harder to Approve
stories.excerpt: California voters will decide in November if the state will make it more difficult for cities to raise local taxes similar to Los Angeles’ 3-year-old Measure ULA “mansion tax” via the ballot box. State lawmakers on Thursday advanced a measure that would require some special taxes to receive support from two-thirds of voters, rather than a simple majority, Bloomberg reported. However, the new ballot measure is slimmer than an earlier campaign because it keeps in place approved taxes that previously passed with a simple majority, including L.A.’s ULA transfer tax on commercial real estate deals. The new stipulations are a compromise between Democratic lawmakers and anti-tax advocates, including the Howard Jarvis Taxpayers Association, which had been backing a more sweeping statewide measure to roll back local tax authority. L.A. voters approved Measure ULA in 2022, and it has become one of the most closely watched and controversial tax policies in the country. Though branded as a “mansion tax,” it applies to all real estate deals a 4 percent tax to property sales above $5.3 million and a 5.5 percent tax to sales of $10.6 million or more. Measure ULA has so far raised about $1.2 billion — less than half of the $2.7 billion projected by early 2026 — for affordable housing production, tenant rental assistance, education and eviction defense. However, last month, the RAND Corporation reported that ULA has cut high-value deals in the city of L.A. by 31 percent, and it has reduced production of large multifamily developments by 30 percent. (The L.A. City Council recently advanced a plan to exempt newly built multifamily buildings that are sold within 10 years of construction.) Anti-tax advocates argue Measure ULA exposed a loophole in the state’s tax law: Under Proposition 218, special taxes require two-thirds approval when placed on the ballot by a government agency, but citizen-led tax initiatives have been allowed to pass with a simple majority. The November measure woul
bullets: California ballot measure would require two-thirds voter approval for new special taxes (not simple majority).
LA's Measure ULA transfer tax (2022) exempted; applies only to future tax measures.
Compromise between Democratic lawmakers and anti-tax groups; commercial real estate transfer taxes remain in flux.
stories.summary: California state lawmakers advanced a ballot measure for November 2026 that would raise the voter approval threshold for new special taxes from a simple majority to two-thirds, though previously approved taxes—including Los Angeles' controversial Measure ULA commercial real estate transfer tax—are grandfathered in. The measure represents a compromise between Democratic legislators and anti-tax advocates including the Howard Jarvis Taxpayers Association.
stories.impact: For multifamily developers in California, especially Los Angeles, this does NOT change the current Measure ULA transfer tax regime (which remains in effect at ~4.5% on commercial real estate transactions above $5M). However, the two-thirds threshold makes future local tax hikes (property taxes, SDCs, affordable housing fees) harder to pass, which may limit municipalities' ability to fund infrastructure that supports new construction. Conversely, reduced future tax pressure could lower overall project costs. Monitor LA and San Francisco for real estate transfer tax ballot language; any effort to broaden or increase ULA would face a higher approval bar going forward, potentially stabilizing development economics long-term.

stories.title: Emergency declaration pushes Philly affordable project forward
stories.excerpt: Intervention from the mayor is finally pushing a significant affordable housing project forward in Philadelphia. The Philadelphia Housing Authority plans to start demolishing dozens of aging buildings in Bartram Village next month after an emergency declaration was issued by Mayor Cherelle Parker, WHYY reported. The declaration clears the way for the razing of 44 buildings, which was held up by a federal review process. The relevant properties in the Southwest Philadelphia complex have been vacant since last year, awaiting redevelopment. Since then, they’ve been taken over by squatters despite the city’s efforts to increase security and board up properties. “This […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Philadelphia Housing Authority green-lit Bartram Village demolition; 44 buildings, 514 replacement public housing units.
22-acre Southwest Philadelphia site redeveloped as $425M project; second phase underway.
Emergency declaration by Mayor Cherelle Parker accelerates 2+ year timeline; residents have right of return.
stories.summary: Philadelphia Mayor Cherelle Parker's emergency declaration cleared the way for the Philadelphia Housing Authority to demolish 44 vacant buildings at Bartram Village in Southwest Philadelphia. The 22-acre site will be redeveloped with 514 units of public housing as part of a $425 million comprehensive redevelopment. This is phase two of a multi-phase project dating to the 1940s; existing residents retain the right to return.
stories.impact: In Southwest Philadelphia, a large public housing redevelopment will generate new rental demand and neighborhood stabilization, but primarily for affordable/public housing operators, not market-rate multifamily developers. However, successful completion of Bartram Village may unlock secondary demand: returning residents and job creation from the project could support nearby market-rate apartments and mixed-use development in surrounding neighborhoods. Monitor the project for catalytic effects on adjacent corridors; post-demolition site clean-up and improved perception may open nearby sites for private development partnerships or adjacency plays.

stories.title: An Evening of Cocktails and Celebration at CO’s 2026 Power Young Professionals Gala
stories.excerpt: While enjoying a beautiful summer evening atop New York City’s famed 120 Broadway, home of the Banker’s Club, Commercial Observer raised its glass Thursday evening to the honorees of this year’s Power Young Professionals awards. This year, CO highlighted 75 outstanding young professionals who have been making their mark on the real estate industry as brokers, finance leaders, developers, architects and more. Those honorees joined CO’s leaders, editors and reporters to celebrate the wins of their career so far and pump them up for what is still to come. CO Editor-in-Chief Max Gross welcomed everyone to the event, reminiscing on the very first iteration of Young Professionals and how far the issue, now a staff favorite, has come. “Young Professionals will always have a place in my heart,” Gross said. “In my first week as editor at Commercial Observer, our former business director — Robyn Reiss — comes up to me and says, ‘Next month we have a 30-under-30 list — which is basically 30 brokers under 30 years old that we’re shining a spotlight on. Good luck.’” Gross had only two weeks to pull the issue together — interviews, photo shoots, the whole shebang — and it was a scramble, but he and his staff got it done. “The CO staff at the time was half the size and we were frantic. It really did feel like a trial by fire,” Gross added. “But we put our heads down and we did it. And now this list is 75 names. Now we draw from the world of finance, construction, architecture and engineering. It’s a far more well-oiled machine.” Some of the Young Professionals in attendance on Thursday night included Thomas Swartz, senior vice president in brokerage at JLL. Swartz is a member of one of the firm’s most active leasing teams and has worked on the repositioning of Piedmont Realty Trust’s 60 Broad Street, a 1.1 million-square-foot office tower in Lower Manhattan. His team also oversees around 60 million square feet, 15 million square feet of which he personally supports. Joining Swartz
bullets: Commercial Observer honored 75 'Power Young Professionals' at gala in New York City.
Event celebrated brokers, finance leaders, developers, architects in real estate industry.
No material impact to multifamily market fundamentals; industry networking and recognition event only.
stories.summary: Commercial Observer hosted its annual Power Young Professionals awards gala at 120 Broadway in Manhattan, honoring 75 emerging real estate professionals including brokers, finance leaders, developers, and architects. The event celebrated career achievements and industry networking.
stories.impact: This is an industry celebration with no direct bearing on multifamily demand, supply, capital, or cost. Skip for market analysis purposes.

stories.title: Korean Chef Jiho Kim to Open Flagship Restaurant at 21 West 17th Street
stories.excerpt: Korean chef Jiho Kim has leased 3,120 square feet at 21 West 17th Street in Manhattan’s Flatiron District for his namesake flagship restaurant, Jiho. The New American eatery with a Korean-inspired menu will take up 1,868 square feet on the ground floor and 1,252 square feet of basement space at the Flatiron location between Fifth Avenue and Avenue of the Americas, Commercial Observer has learned. Asking rent was $120 per square foot. Carson Shahrabani and Noam Aziz of Meridian Capital Group negotiated on behalf of Kim in the transaction. “Jiho has been searching for his flagship location restaurant, and it was a pleasure to finally find the perfect site for him after showing him spaces for years,” Shahrabani told CO. Kim is the founder of the West Village restaurant Joomak and Olly Olly Market food hall restaurant Ddobar in West Chelsea. Joomak is temporarily closed, according to Yelp, and DDOBAR officially closed its doors in March of this year. The midblock address at 21 West 17th Street is within Louie, a two-building condominium complex developed by Vinbaytel Property Development. The building, completed in July 2024, includes 21 full-floor condo units. Its second retail space, located on the north side of the block at 16 West 18th Street, was leased to chef Andrew Sargent for his first New York City restaurant earlier this month. Ripco Real Estate’s Ben Davis and Alex Svetlakou represented the landlord. A spokesperson for the team did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Korean chef Jiho Kim leases 3,120-sf at 21 West 17th, Manhattan; asking rent $120/sf.
Ground floor (1,868 sf) and basement (1,252 sf) flagship restaurant in Flatiron District.
Prior concepts (Joomak, DDOBAR) closed; third restaurant attempt signals market recovery and chef persistence.
stories.summary: Chef Jiho Kim leased 3,120 square feet at 21 West 17th Street in Manhattan's Flatiron District for his New American/Korean-inspired flagship restaurant 'Jiho.' The space spans ground floor and basement at $120/sf asking rent. Kim is also founder of prior restaurants Joomak (temporarily closed) and DDOBAR in West Chelsea (closed March 2024).
stories.impact: Restaurant leasing in Flatiron/Chelsea at $120/sf demonstrates persistent demand for food-service ground-floor retail in premium Manhattan neighborhoods, supporting the long-term viability of mixed-use development with restaurant components. For multifamily developers, this validates the feasibility of ground-floor retail in high-barrier-to-entry submarkets. However, Kim's prior closures suggest the concept-execution risk in NYC food service remains high; use this as a signal that ground-floor retail economics depend on strong operator credentials and brand, not just location. Apartment projects with ground-floor retail in these neighborhoods remain attractive but require careful retail tenant selection.

stories.title: Manhattan Subleasing Tightens as AI Firms Fuel Office Growth in Q2
stories.excerpt: Artificial intelligence and technology companies have been driving demand for office space in Manhattan during the second quarter of 2026, helping to soften the city’s post-pandemic sublease glut, according to preliminary second-quarter data from JLL. Manhattan’s sublease inventory has fallen below 11 million square feet, according to JLL, a steep drop from the late-2022 peak of approximately 23 million square feet of sublease space available. That decline is the result of an increase in leasing activity by AI companies, JLL found (although law firms have taken a rather large chunk of space so far in 2026 as well). So far this year, AI firms have signed 21 deals totaling 719,200 square feet in Manhattan, a number that is on track to overtake last year’s total of 845,000 square feet. About 70 percent of the leases signed were in the Midtown South market, which is evolving into a “tech and innovation core” in the city. Some of those Midtown South deals included AI sales platform Clay relocating to 163,095 square feet at 11 Madison Avenue in March and AI-driven advertising firm Agentio taking 20,000 square feet at 295 Fifth Avenue, also in March. “Manhattan’s office market gained momentum throughout the second quarter as demand continued to concentrate in high-quality space,” Jamie Katcher, executive managing director at JLL, said in a statement. “The most notable development is how quickly the sublease overhang has been absorbed,” Katcher added. “Growth from AI firms, as well as financial services companies and law firms, is driving long-term leasing decisions, while landlords are increasingly pulling back available sublease space to pursue direct deals at substantially higher rents.” Overall, office availability across Manhattan dipped by 50 basis points to 12.7 percent on a quarter-over-quarter basis, according to JLL’s data, meaning there was about 2 million square feet less space available in the second quarter than in the first three month
bullets: Manhattan sublease inventory fell below 11M sf (from 23M in late 2022) as AI/tech firms signed deals.
AI companies signed 21 deals for 719,200 sf in Q2 2026; 70% in Midtown South.
AI/tech demand plus law firm leasing (Alston & Bird, etc.) driving office absorption; Midtown South emerging as 'tech core.'
stories.summary: Manhattan's sublease inventory dropped below 11 million square feet in Q2 2026, down sharply from the 23 million-sf peak in late 2022, driven by strong leasing by AI and tech companies. AI firms signed 21 deals totaling 719,200 square feet, with 70% concentrated in Midtown South. Law firms have also contributed significantly to absorption. Midtown South is evolving into a 'tech and innovation core' for the city.
stories.impact: In Manhattan, the structural reduction in sublease supply (post-pandemic desk-sharing and return-to-office adjustment) combined with new demand from AI/tech startups is reshaping office geography. Midtown South is becoming a high-demand submarket for technology talent, which will attract young, highly-paid professionals seeking rental housing. For multifamily developers, this signals a demographic shift toward younger, tech-sector renters in specific Manhattan corridors (Midtown South, Flatiron, parts of the East Side). Apartments targeting this cohort (studio, 1-BR, 2-BR at $4K–$6K/mo) in or near tech hubs will benefit from job growth and reduced office sublease competition for landlords. Consider acquiring or developing rental housing near Midtown South office clusters to capture this demand wave.

stories.title: Judge rules in favor of Inglewood in legal dispute with Hollywood Park
stories.excerpt: City of Inglewood: 1. Stan Kroenke and his SoFi Stadium: 0. The city surrounding the Hollywood Park retail, office residential and stadium complex has prevailed in one of two lawsuits filed by its billionaire developer and owner during what was supposed to have been a triumph during this year’s World Cup tournament, Politico reported. Hollywood Park, which includes the $5 billion stadium that is host to eight World Cup matches, sued the city after it struck a deal last year with Los Angeles-based WOW Media to plant digital billboards across Inglewood and near the stadium at 1001 South Stadium Drive.  […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: LA Superior Court ruled in favor of Inglewood city over Hollywood Park (SoFi Stadium owner) on billboard deal.
Judge upheld city's long-term WOW Media digital billboard agreement; reimbursement lawsuit ($400M) still pending.
Court rejected Hollywood Park's claim that voter-approved development agreement was unenforceable.
stories.summary: A Los Angeles Superior Court judge ruled in favor of the City of Inglewood in a dispute with Hollywood Park (owner of SoFi Stadium and surrounding 300-acre urban village) over digital billboards. The court upheld Inglewood's long-term agreement with WOW Media for digital billboard placement, rejecting Hollywood Park's claim that the voter-approved agreement was unenforceable. A separate $400 million reimbursement lawsuit filed by Hollywood Park remains pending.
stories.impact: In Inglewood/South Bay, the court decision stabilizes the city's revenue streams from commercial signage, reducing Hollywood Park's leverage to extract concessions or reimbursements. For multifamily developers considering projects in or near Inglewood, this signals a city government with stable, diversified revenue sources and a willingness to litigate to protect municipal interests. This reduces risk of unexpected fee hikes or special assessments on new development, but also suggests Inglewood will aggressively enforce development agreements. Monitor the $400M reimbursement case; if Hollywood Park prevails, it could signal higher-than-expected infrastructure cost-sharing burdens on future Inglewood projects.

stories.title: NADG Lands $120M to Build Rental on Farmland in Palm Beach County
stories.excerpt: North American Development Group (NADG) has secured a $120 million construction loan for a multifamily development on agricultural land near Delray Beach, Fla. Wells Fargo provided the financing for a 476-unit development, called NUVO Delray Beach, at 9494 Ilumina Way, located just south of Atlantic Avenue, about a mile and a half west of Florida’s Turnpike. The garden-style project will rise on 33 acres, 12 acres of which will be reserved for outdoor recreation, including a bird sanctuary. Construction got underway this week and is expected to be completed by late 2028. Four years ago, the West Palm Beach-based developer paid just under $42 million for the parcel, The South Florida Business Journal reported. The development sits within Palm Beach County’s 22,000-acre Agricultural Reserve, a protected farming region. In recent years, public officials have approved residential projects on the land, though mostly single-family homes developments. Some developments have been met with criticism. Last year, farmer Richard Bowman proposed building townhomes and workforce housing on nearby land, spanning about 75 acres, which environmentalists and some county commissioners opposed, the Palm Beach Post reported. So far, no construction has occurred, per property records. A representative for NADG did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: NADG secures $120M Wells Fargo construction loan for 476-unit NUVO Delray Beach; 33 acres.
Garden-style development on agricultural land near Highway Turnpike; completion late 2028.
Sits within Palm Beach County 22,000-acre Agricultural Reserve; NADG paid ~$42M for land 4 years ago.
stories.summary: North American Development Group (NADG) secured a $120 million construction loan from Wells Fargo for NUVO Delray Beach, a 476-unit garden-style multifamily development on 33 acres of agricultural land near Delray Beach, Florida. Located at 9494 Ilumina Way, about 1.5 miles west of Florida's Turnpike, the project began construction this week with completion expected in late 2028. NADG acquired the land approximately four years ago for just under $42 million.
stories.impact: In Palm Beach County, the approval and financing of a large multifamily project on Agricultural Reserve land signals municipality willingness to convert farmland to residential use to address housing demand. The $120M construction facility at a 476-unit project implies healthy construction lender appetite for South Florida rental development (roughly $252K per unit). For developers, this validates the Palm Beach County apartment market's strength and lender confidence in Delray Beach rents and absorption. However, agricultural conversion projects face increasing regulatory scrutiny (environmental review, local opposition); NADG's success may accelerate competing farmland conversion proposals or trigger more restrictive zoning. Acquisitions of Agricultural Reserve land should factor in extended entitlement timelines and community opposition risk.

stories.title: Habitat for Humanity shifts focus to outer boroughs
stories.excerpt: A renowned not-for-profit organization is expanding its output into New York City’s outer boroughs. Habitat for Humanity is constructing a total of 179 co-op units across Brooklyn and the Bronx, the Commercial Observer reported. The separate developments represent the largest influx for the New York chapter’s pipeline. In Brooklyn, Habitat is building Constellation, a community that spans eight sites in East New York, Ocean Hill, Brownsville, Bedford-Stuyvesant and Rugby Remsen Village, Brooklyn Paper reported. Originally envisioned for 77 units, the organization has since grown the project out to 108 units. Similarly, Claremont House in the Bronx jumped from a 40-unit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Habitat for Humanity building 179 co-op units across Bronx and Brooklyn; largest pipeline influx.
Brooklyn: Constellation project scaled from 77 to 108 units across 8 East NY/Brownsville sites.
Bronx: Claremont House grew from 40 to 71 units; funded via City of Yes, Open Door, New Infill programs.
stories.summary: Habitat for Humanity's New York chapter is constructing 179 cooperative ownership units across Brooklyn and the Bronx—its largest pipeline expansion. In Brooklyn, the Constellation project spans eight sites in East New York, Brownsville, Bed-Stuy, and other neighborhoods, scaled from 77 to 108 units. In the Bronx, Claremont House grew from 40 to 71 units. Projects are funded via NYC's City of Yes for Housing, Open Door Program, and New Infill Housing Opportunity Program; land is controlled via 99-year Community Land Trust leases.
stories.impact: In Brooklyn and the Bronx, affordable housing (cooperative ownership model) production is accelerating via city subsidy and zoning liberalization (City of Yes). While Habitat's co-op model differs from market-rate rental multifamily, the project signal indicates: (1) strong municipal commitment to affordable housing in outer-borough gentrification corridors (East NY, Brownsville, Bed-Stuy), and (2) availability of CLT land and city funding for housing. For market-rate multifamily developers, Habitat's success in assembling multi-site portfolios and scaling projects suggests demand and feasibility in these neighborhoods. However, the flood of affordable housing supply may cap rent growth and increase competitive intensity for market-rate projects. Focus on value-add and workforce housing (100–150% AMI) as a niche market-rate segment.

stories.title: Houston Rodeo partially breaks from NRG Park with $300M livestock campus
stories.excerpt: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is partially bucking NRG Park to build its own $300 million facility about five miles east.  The Rodeo is building a 1 million-square-foot complex at the corner of Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard. Part of the project will be the construction of their own arena, but some operations will remain at NRG Park. The Rodeo is working on a new lease with the Houston Texans as well as Harris County, according to the Houston Business Journal.  Houston Rodeo president and CEO Chris Boleman told the outlet that this is not a relocation, even though […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo building $300M, 1M-sf facility near Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard.
New complex features livestock barns, auction hall, sales center; some events remain at NRG Park.
Part of centennial celebration (2032); Populous designed; Clay Development GC; construction underway.
stories.summary: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is building a $300 million, 1 million-square-foot facility at the intersection of Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard, about five miles east of NRG Park. The complex includes livestock barns, an auction hall, sales convention center, and outdoor cattle yard to host year-round agricultural events, though the marquee rodeo and concerts remain at NRG. Construction is underway, designed by Populous and managed by Clay Development & Construction.
stories.impact: In the Houston area, the Rodeo's partial relocation and capital investment signals growth in regional agricultural/event infrastructure and economic vitality around Highway 288 East. For multifamily developers, this anchors long-term foot traffic and event-driven demand in an area with limited current density; the $300M facility may catalyze adjacent mixed-use and residential development (hotels, apartments) serving visitors and workers. Monitor for concurrent infrastructure upgrades (roads, utilities, parking) that could open nearby land for multifamily projects. However, the facility's agricultural/event focus may limit year-round employment and renter base; pair apartment development with other job anchors.

stories.title: Developer looks to bring almost 300 units to Flushing
stories.excerpt: Flushing is poised to receive an influx of nearly 300 units — many affordable — should Gary Chen secure a zoning change. On Monday, Chen filed an application with the New York City Department of City Planning for a mixed-use project at 32-02 Linden Place in the Queens neighborhood, the Commercial Observer reported. The zoning change would be bound by 32nd Avenue, Linden Place and Farrington Street. Chen wants to build a 10-story, 296-unit complex at the site, which would also include 33,000 square feet of commercial space and nearly 200 parking spaces. Roughly 25 percent of the units would […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Gary Chen files zoning change for 296-unit, 10-story development at 32-02 Linden Place, Flushing.
25% affordable units (74 units); 33,000-sf commercial; ~200 parking spaces.
Chen acquired assemblage (Linden Place + Farrington Street industrial) in 2024 for $26.5M total.
stories.summary: Developer Gary Chen submitted a zoning change application to the NYC Department of City Planning for a 296-unit, 10-story mixed-use project at 32-02 Linden Place in Flushing, Queens. The project includes 33,000 square feet of commercial space and nearly 200 parking spaces, with approximately 25% of units designated affordable. Chen completed land assembly in 2024 for $26.5 million total.
stories.impact: In Flushing, Queens, the zoning application signals continued multifamily development momentum fueled by transit access and demographic demand. Chen's $26.5M land cost implies ~$90K/unit raw land basis for a 296-unit project—reasonable in Flushing. The 25% affordable mandate (likely via mandatory inclusionary housing) is routine for NYC; underwrite accordingly. For developers, Flushing's pipeline remains robust; Chen's application, along with concurrent filings in Kew Gardens (Grun Group, 108 units) and Ditmars (26 units), suggest a competitive submarket with multiple land assemblages in entitlement. Monitor approval timelines and neighboring projects' absorption to gauge rent growth and lease-up velocity; Flushing rents may moderate if multiple large projects deliver simultaneously.

stories.title: Dallas secures $19M worth of incentives for potential Morgan Stanley move
stories.excerpt: Dallas City Council greenlit an $18.5 million incentive package Wednesday for Morgan Stanley’s potential Y’all Street move.  The financial services and investment bank will spend a little time in Downtown Dallas before moving to Uptown in 2031. The package coincides with Morgan Stanley earmarking $700 million for its new digs at 2401 McKinney Avenue, which will bring hundreds of employees to Dallas’s burgeoning financial hub.  Morgan Stanley has yet to formally communicate their plans, but documents filed with the city indicate the timeline, according to the Dallas Business Journal. Morgan Stanley can still opt out and head to Alpharetta, Georgia, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Dallas City Council approves $18.5M incentive package for Morgan Stanley; $700M HQ investment planned.
Morgan Stanley targets 2401 McKinney Avenue in Uptown (700,000-sf, ~3,800 employees); occupancy 2031.
Temp office: 255,000-sf at downtown Fountain Place before 2031 move; Trammell Crow developing building.
stories.summary: Dallas City Council greenlit an $18.5 million incentive package for Morgan Stanley's potential relocation to Dallas. The investment bank plans a $700 million campus at 2401 McKinney Avenue in Uptown (700,000 square feet, approximately 3,800 employees), targeted for occupancy in 2031. Until the Uptown building is ready, Morgan Stanley will occupy 255,000 square feet at downtown Fountain Place. Trammell Crow Company is developing the Uptown project with a $650 million contribution (total ~$1.3 billion). Construction begins fall 2026.
stories.impact: In Dallas, Morgan Stanley's relocation (contingent on formal commitment) would represent a transformational job influx: ~3,800 high-wage financial services employees moving to the Uptown submarket starting 2031. This drives: (1) renter demand in Dallas/Uptown multifamily; (2) supporting retail and hospitality; (3) potential spillover job creation (professional services, dining). For multifamily developers, this signals a 5-year window to acquire or develop apartments near the McKinney Avenue site (positioned for 2028–2031 delivery to capture lease-up as employees arrive). Dallas's financial services sector is consolidating; Morgan Stanley's move validates Uptown as a growing financial hub. However, Morgan Stanley can still opt for Alpharetta, GA—deal is not yet final. Monitor for formal commitment; if secured, Uptown apartment rents and absorption rates will likely accelerate.
